questions.stem: The symmetric group $S_n$ is defined as which set, under composition?
options: The set of all functions from $\{1, \dots, n\}$ to itself.
The set of all subsets of $\{1, 2, \dots, n\}$.
The set of all bijections from $\{1, 2, \dots, n\}$ to itself.
questions.explanation: $S_n$ is the group of all permutations of $\{1, 2, \dots, n\}$, that is all bijections of the set to itself, with composition as the operation. Arbitrary functions need not be invertible, so only the bijections form a group.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: What is the order $|S_n|$ of the symmetric group on $n$ symbols?
questions.explanation: There are $n!$ ways to arrange $n$ symbols, so $S_n$ has $n!$ elements. For instance $|S_3| = 3! = 6$ and $|S_4| = 4! = 24$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: What is the order of the symmetric group $S_4$?
questions.explanation: $|S_4| = 4! = 4 \cdot 3 \cdot 2 \cdot 1 = 24$. These are all the permutations of four symbols.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: In cycle notation, what does the cycle $(1\ 3\ 5)$ mean as a permutation?
options: It sends each of $1, 3, 5$ to itself.
It sends $1 \to 3$, $3 \to 5$, $5 \to 1$, fixing all other symbols.
questions.explanation: The cycle $(1\ 3\ 5)$ moves each listed symbol to the next, cyclically: $1 \to 3$, $3 \to 5$, $5 \to 1$, and everything not listed stays fixed. Reading it the other way around would describe its inverse.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: The order of a permutation written as a product of disjoint cycles equals which quantity?
options: The product of the cycle lengths.
The length of the longest cycle.
The sum of the cycle lengths.
The least common multiple of the cycle lengths.
questions.explanation: For a permutation in disjoint cycle form, the order is the least common multiple of the cycle lengths. Each cycle of length $k$ returns to start after $k$ applications, and disjoint cycles act independently, so the whole permutation returns to the identity after $\text{lcm}$ of the lengths.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: What is the order of the permutation $(1\ 2\ 3)(4\ 5)$ in $S_5$?
questions.explanation: The disjoint cycles have lengths $3$ and $2$, so the order is $\text{lcm}(3, 2) = 6$. The 3-cycle repeats every $3$ steps and the transposition every $2$ steps; both realign after $6$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: What is the order of the permutation $(1\ 2\ 3\ 4)(5\ 6\ 7)$ in $S_7$?
questions.explanation: The cycle lengths are $4$ and $3$, which are coprime, so the order is $\text{lcm}(4, 3) = 12$. This permutation generates a cyclic subgroup of order $12$ in $S_7$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: What is the order of the permutation $(1\ 2)(3\ 4\ 5\ 6)$ in $S_6$?
questions.explanation: The disjoint cycle lengths are $2$ and $4$, so the order is $\text{lcm}(2, 4) = 4$. Note it is the least common multiple, not the product, so the answer is $4$ rather than $8$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: A transposition is a cycle of which length?
questions.explanation: A transposition is a 2-cycle, swapping two symbols and fixing the rest, for example $(1\ 2)$. Every permutation can be written as a product of transpositions, which is the basis for defining the sign of a permutation.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: A permutation is called even when it can be written as a product of how many transpositions?
questions.explanation: A permutation is even if it is a product of an even number of transpositions, and odd if it needs an odd number. Although a permutation has many transposition factorizations, the parity of the count is always the same, so even and odd are well defined.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: A single $k$-cycle can be written as a product of how many transpositions, and hence has which parity?
questions.explanation: A $k$-cycle factors as $k - 1$ transpositions, for example $(1\ 2\ 3\ 4) = (1\ 4)(1\ 3)(1\ 2)$. So a $k$-cycle is even exactly when $k - 1$ is even, that is when $k$ is odd. Thus 3-cycles are even and transpositions (2-cycles) are odd.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Is the 3-cycle $(1\ 2\ 3)$ an even or odd permutation?
options: It depends on the surrounding group $S_n$.
questions.explanation: A 3-cycle equals $k - 1 = 2$ transpositions: $(1\ 2\ 3) = (1\ 3)(1\ 2)$. Two is even, so $(1\ 2\ 3)$ is an even permutation. Parity is intrinsic to the permutation and does not change with $n$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Is the permutation $(1\ 2\ 3)(4\ 5)$ even or odd?
questions.explanation: The 3-cycle contributes $2$ transpositions and the 2-cycle contributes $1$, for a total of $3$ transpositions. Three is odd, so the permutation is odd. Equivalently, even times odd is odd.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: The alternating group $A_n$ consists of which permutations, and has what order for $n \ge 2$?
questions.explanation: $A_n$ is the set of even permutations in $S_n$. It is a subgroup of index $2$, so $|A_n| = n!/2$ for $n \ge 2$. The even permutations are closed under composition and inverses, while the odd ones are not (they lack the identity).
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: What is the order of the alternating group $A_4$?
questions.explanation: $|A_4| = 4!/2 = 24/2 = 12$. The alternating group on four symbols has twelve elements, exactly the even permutations of $S_4$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Two disjoint cycles (cycles with no symbols in common) satisfy which property under composition?
options: Their composition is always a single cycle.
questions.explanation: Disjoint cycles commute, because they move separate sets of symbols and so do not interfere. This is why a permutation written in disjoint cycle form is unambiguous regardless of the order the cycles are listed.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Using the convention that the rightmost permutation acts first, what is the product $(1\ 3)(1\ 2)$ in $S_3$?
questions.explanation: Apply $(1\ 2)$ first, then $(1\ 3)$. Tracking each symbol: $1 \to 2 \to 2$, $2 \to 1 \to 3$, $3 \to 3 \to 1$. So $1 \to 2$, $2 \to 3$, $3 \to 1$, which is the 3-cycle $(1\ 2\ 3)$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: What is the inverse of the cycle $(1\ 2\ 3\ 4)$?
questions.explanation: The inverse of a cycle reverses the order of its entries: $(1\ 2\ 3\ 4)^{-1} = (4\ 3\ 2\ 1) = (1\ 4\ 3\ 2)$. Check: the original sends $1 \to 2$, and the inverse sends $2 \to 1$, as required.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: What is the order of the permutation $(1\ 2\ 3\ 4\ 5)$ in $S_5$?
questions.explanation: A single 5-cycle has order equal to its length, $5$. Applying it five times returns every symbol to its starting position, and no smaller positive power does.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: What is the order of the permutation $(1\ 2\ 3)(4\ 5\ 6\ 7)$ in $S_7$?
questions.explanation: The cycle lengths are $3$ and $4$, so the order is $\text{lcm}(3, 4) = 12$ since $3$ and $4$ are coprime. The lcm, not the sum or the larger length, gives the order.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Is the identity permutation even or odd?
options: Even, as a product of zero transpositions.
Neither, parity does not apply to it.
questions.explanation: The identity is the empty product of transpositions, and zero is even, so the identity is an even permutation. It therefore always lies in the alternating group $A_n$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Within $S_6$, what is the largest possible order of any single element?
questions.explanation: The order of an element of $S_6$ is the lcm of cycle lengths in a partition of $6$. Checking partitions: $6 \to 6$, $3+3 \to 3$, $2+4 \to 4$, $1+2+3 \to 6$, $2+2+2 \to 2$. The maximum lcm is $6$, achieved for instance by a 6-cycle. (A larger value like $12$ would need lengths summing to more than $6$.)
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: The product of two odd permutations is which of the following?
questions.explanation: Parity adds like integers mod $2$: odd plus odd is even. Concretely, an odd permutation is a product of an odd number of transpositions, and combining two such gives an even total. This is why the even permutations, not the odd ones, form the subgroup $A_n$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: How many elements does the alternating group $A_5$ contain?
questions.explanation: $|A_5| = 5!/2 = 120/2 = 60$. The alternating group on five symbols has sixty elements and is famously the smallest non-abelian simple group.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Consider the permutation $\sigma = (1\ 2\ 3)(4\ 5)$ in $S_5$. Find its order, and state whether it is an even or odd permutation.
questions.solution: The disjoint cycle lengths are $3$ and $2$, so the order is $\text{lcm}(3,2)$, which equals {{1}}. The 3-cycle is $2$ transpositions and the 2-cycle is $1$, giving $3$ transpositions in total, so $\sigma$ is {{2}}.
questions.explanation: The order is $\text{lcm}(3,2) = 6$. The transposition count is $(3-1) + (2-1) = 3$, which is odd, so $\sigma$ is an odd permutation.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Work in the symmetric group $S_4$. State its order $|S_4|$, and the order of the alternating subgroup $A_4$.
questions.solution: The number of permutations of four symbols is $4!$, so $|S_4|$ equals {{1}}. The alternating group consists of the even permutations and has half as many elements, so $|A_4|$ equals {{2}}.
questions.explanation: $|S_4| = 4! = 24$, and $|A_4| = 24/2 = 12$, since exactly half of the permutations are even.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Consider the permutation $\tau = (1\ 2\ 3\ 4)(5\ 6)$ in $S_6$. Find its order, and the number of transpositions in a factorization (to determine parity).
questions.solution: The cycle lengths are $4$ and $2$, so the order is $\text{lcm}(4,2)$, which equals {{1}}. The 4-cycle uses $3$ transpositions and the 2-cycle uses $1$, for a total of {{2}} transpositions, making $\tau$ an even permutation.
questions.explanation: The order is $\text{lcm}(4,2) = 4$. The transposition count is $(4-1) + (2-1) = 3 + 1 = 4$, which is even, so $\tau$ is even.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Using the convention that the rightmost factor acts first, compute the product $(1\ 2)(2\ 3)$ in $S_3$, expressed as a single cycle, and give the order of the resulting permutation.
questions.solution: Applying $(2\ 3)$ first then $(1\ 2)$: $1 \to 1 \to 2$, $2 \to 3 \to 3$, $3 \to 2 \to 1$, so the product is the 3-cycle that sends $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 1$. Being a 3-cycle, its order is {{1}}, and as a product of an even number of transpositions it is {{2}}.
questions.explanation: Tracking symbols gives $(1\ 2)(2\ 3) = (1\ 2\ 3)$. A 3-cycle has order $3$, and it is a product of $2$ transpositions, an even number, so it is an even permutation.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra
